content.overview:
Emergencies — natural disasters (floods, cyclones, earthquakes), conflicts, and pandemics — significantly increase the prevalence of mental health conditions in affected populations. WHO estimates that approximately 1 in 5 people in conflict-affected areas experience depression, anxiety, PTSD, bipolar disorder, or schizophrenia, with around 1 in 11 having a moderate-to-severe condition. India regularly faces emergencies including floods (Kerala, Assam, Bihar), cyclones (Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal), earthquakes, and was significantly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic's mental health impact. Mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) is now recognised as a critical component of emergency response.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This information is for health awareness only. Strong emotional reactions after an emergency are normal and usually improve with time and support. If symptoms persist, worsen, or interfere with daily functioning — especially for children — seek support. Tele-MANAS (14416) provides free, confidential support 24x7, including during disasters.
types: Acute stress reactions: Normal responses to abnormal events — fear, shock, grief, sleep disturbances (usually resolve within weeks)
Adjustment disorders: Difficulty adapting to the changed circumstances after a disaster
PTSD (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder): Persistent re-experiencing, avoidance, hyperarousal (see PTSD topic)
Depression and anxiety disorders: Often triggered or worsened by loss, displacement, uncertainty
Exacerbation of pre-existing mental illness: Disruption of medication/care access worsens existing conditions
Substance use: Increased risk as a coping mechanism
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
symptoms: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
warning_signs: Common (often normal) reactions in early days:
Grief reactions to loss of loved ones, homes, livelihoods
Symptoms persisting beyond a month and worsening
Inability to perform daily functions (self-care, caring for children)
Suicidal thoughts — see Suicide topic for immediate resources
In children: Regression (bedwetting, clinginess), nightmares, behavioural changes, refusal to be separated from caregivers
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Direct exposure to disaster (injury, loss of life of loved ones)
Loss of home, livelihood, or community
Children and elderly are particularly vulnerable
prevention: Psychological First Aid (PFA): Basic, humane support — listening, providing comfort, connecting people to information and services; does NOT require mental health professional training
Maintaining routines where possible, especially for children
Providing accurate information to reduce uncertainty and rumours
Protecting vulnerable groups (unaccompanied children, elderly, disabled) in relief camps
Avoiding re-traumatisation (e.g. repeatedly asking survivors to recount traumatic events without purpose)
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
environmental_control: Safe, dignified shelter conditions in relief camps (privacy, security, separate facilities for women)
Continuity of care for people with pre-existing mental illness (ensuring medication supply continues)
vaccination: Not directly applicable; but routine immunisation continuity is important during emergencies (cholera, measles outbreak risk in displacement settings)
diagnosis: Most acute reactions do not need formal diagnosis — supportive care is sufficient
Screening for those with persistent/severe symptoms by trained personnel
District Mental Health Programme teams can be deployed for disaster response
treatment_awareness_only: Psychological First Aid (PFA): First-line response by trained volunteers/community workers
Community-based psychosocial support: Group support, community activities, restoring social connections
Clinical care: For persistent/severe conditions — referral to mental health professionals (psychotherapy, medication if needed)
Continuity of medication: For those with pre-existing psychiatric conditions, ensuring medicines are not interrupted during displacement
Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks and worsening
Inability to care for self or children
Any suicidal thoughts — immediate help via Tele-MANAS (14416)
Children showing significant behavioural regression or persistent distress
Seek medical care for severe, persistent, worsening, or red-flag symptoms. Use emergency number 112 for life-threatening situations.
india_specific_context: NDMA (National Disaster Management Authority) includes psychosocial support in disaster response guidelines
District Mental Health Programme teams can be mobilised during disasters
Tele-MANAS (14416 / 1800-891-4416): 24x7 free helpline, especially valuable during/after disasters when in-person services may be disrupted
NIMHANS has developed disaster mental health response protocols and training materials
After major disasters (e.g. Kerala floods, Cyclone Amphan), state governments have deployed MHPSS teams to relief camps

faqs.question: How do disasters like floods affect mental health in India?
faqs.answer: Disasters cause acute stress reactions, grief, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), depression, and anxiety. Displacement, loss of livelihoods, and community disruption compound mental health burden. Mental health support is an integral part of disaster response in India.

faqs.question: What mental health support is available during disasters in India?
faqs.answer: NIMHANS, government psychiatric teams, and trained counsellors are deployed during major disasters. Tele MANAS (14416) operates 24/7 including during emergencies. NDMA guidelines include psychosocial support as part of disaster response protocols.

faqs.question: Is it normal to feel extremely distressed after a disaster?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fear, sadness, grief, anger, and sleep disturbance are normal responses to abnormal events. Most people recover naturally with time and community support. Seek professional help if distress is severe, persistent beyond 4 weeks, or significantly interferes with daily functioning.

faqs.question: How can community members support each other's mental health after a disaster?
faqs.answer: Be present and listen actively. Share accurate information to counter rumours. Help with practical needs (food, shelter, connectivity). Reconnect people with family and community. Identify those showing severe distress and link them to mental health services. Social support is among the most powerful recovery factors.
